content.text: Evaluating agents is far harder than evaluating single-turn models, and the difficulty is itself a bottleneck on progress: you cannot reliably improve what you cannot reliably measure. Agent tasks are open-ended, multi-step, and often have many valid solutions, which defeats the exact-match metrics that work for classification.

Agent evaluation is the practice of measuring whether an agent accomplishes goals, and how well, across realistic tasks. Unlike a benchmark with a single correct label, an agent may reach a goal by different paths, take side effects along the way, and partially succeed. Evaluation must therefore judge

The field spans several approaches: outcome-based checks (did the task get done?), trajectory analysis (was the path efficient and safe?), and model-based judging (using an LLM to grade open-ended output). Each has failure modes, and no single method suffices, which is why evaluation is an open problem rather than a solved practice.

Evaluation drives everything downstream. Model selection, prompt iteration, safety sign-off, and regression detection all depend on a trustworthy metric. A flawed evaluation silently rewards the wrong behavior — an agent that games the metric — and misdirects months of development effort.

It matters more for agents because the cost of a bad metric compounds. A misleading single-turn benchmark yields a slightly worse model; a misleading agent benchmark can certify an unreliable autonomous system as ready for deployment. As agents take real actions, the gap between measured and actual competence becomes a safety issue.
— how held-out tasks estimate capability and how leaderboards can be gamed.
— precision, recall, and the difference between outcome and process measures.
— using models to grade output, and the biases this introduces.
— why noisy, non-deterministic runs need many samples.

An agent can reach the right answer by luck or a wrong answer by a sound method. Outcome metrics reward the former; process metrics reward the latter. Robust evaluation needs both, because deploying an agent that succeeds unreliably by accident is dangerous.

Some tasks have checkable answers (a passing test suite, a correct number); many valuable tasks — a written report, a design — do not. The lack of a cheap, objective verifier for open-ended output is the central difficulty, and it pushes evaluators toward model-based judging with all its biases.

Public benchmarks leak into training data, inflating scores without reflecting real capability. As a benchmark ages, its measured difficulty drops for reasons unrelated to genuine progress, so fresh, held-out, or dynamically generated tasks are needed.
Because agents sample, a single run is noisy. Comparing two agents requires enough runs to distinguish real differences from sampling variance, using confidence intervals rather than point estimates.

attrs.alt: Agent evaluation pipeline combining outcome, trajectory, and judge scoring

content.text: A trustworthy pipeline never relies on a single run or a single metric. It samples many trajectories, scores each through complementary lenses, and reports distributions so that noise and metric-gaming are visible rather than hidden.
Estimating an agent's true success rate from finite runs is a statistical problem. With
has a confidence interval whose half-width scales as

This is why comparing agents that differ by a few points on a small suite is unsound: the intervals overlap. Halving the margin requires roughly quadrupling the number of runs, which is why rigorous agent evaluation is expensive.
A minimal harness runs each task many times, scores through multiple lenses, and reports rates with intervals.

def llm_judge(task, trajectory, rubric, llm):
    """Score open-ended output against an explicit rubric; mitigate position bias."""
    prompt = (f"Task: {task}\nRubric: {rubric}\n"
              f"Agent output:\n{trajectory.final_output}\n"
              f"Score 0-1 for each rubric criterion and justify briefly. "
              f"Do not reward verbosity or confident tone.")
    raw = llm(prompt)
    return parse_scores(raw)   # average rubric criteria into a single 0..1

Evaluating an agent is like grading an open-ended exam, not a multiple-choice test. There is no answer key for an essay, students who write confidently can fool a careless grader, and a single question cannot rank the class. You need a rubric, several graders, several questions, and a way to catch students who studied last year's leaked paper. Every hard part of agent evaluation has a counterpart in that classroom.
the task has an objective verifier — tests pass, a value matches, a file exists.
efficiency, safety, or method matters, not just the final answer.
output is open-ended and no cheap objective check exists — with a rubric to constrain it.
Use fresh or dynamic tasks when
contamination is a concern for a well-known benchmark.
comparing agents; point estimates on small suites mislead.

Frontier labs increasingly rely on private, held-out evaluation suites and dynamically generated tasks precisely because public benchmarks are contaminated. A realistic setup runs each candidate agent hundreds of times across a fresh task distribution, scores with both automated checkers and rubric-guided judges, and gates release on confidence intervals rather than a single headline number. The engineering cost of this rigour is substantial, which is why weaker teams over-trust cheap public scores.

Rigorous evaluation is compute-heavy: statistical power demands many runs, and each run is a full multi-step agent trajectory. Costs scale with tasks multiplied by runs multiplied by trajectory length. Practical harnesses subsample during development and reserve full-power runs for release decisions, and they cache deterministic sub-results to avoid re-running unchanged components. LLM-judge cost can rival the agent itself, so cheaper judge models are used for routine screening.
— GAIA, WebArena, SWE-bench, and tau-bench target different agent capabilities.
— OpenAI Evals, Inspect, and Braintrust structure task suites and scoring.
— rubric-based LLM-judge libraries with bias-mitigation options.
— trajectory capture that makes failure attribution possible after the run.
Evaluation itself can be attacked or fooled, so guard the metric as carefully as the model.
— agents may exploit a checker's loopholes; audit high-scoring trajectories for shortcuts.
— outputs can be crafted to flatter an LLM judge; use rubrics and adversarial checks.
— keep test sets private and rotate tasks to prevent training-set leakage.
— a benchmark optimised against stops measuring generalisation; hold out a fresh set.

attrs.alt: Attributing an agent failure to the responsible stage of its trajectory

content.text: A single success-rate number tells you an agent failed but not why. Failure attribution decomposes trajectories to localise the responsible stage, turning an opaque score into an actionable diagnosis. Two agents with identical success rates can have completely different failure profiles, and only attribution reveals it.
Because public benchmarks leak into training data, a rising score may reflect memorisation. A canary check and dynamic task generation help distinguish genuine capability from recall.

import hashlib, random

def canary_check(model_fn, canary_string):
    """If the model can reproduce a benchmark's private canary, it saw the test set."""
    probe = f"Complete this exact string if you have seen it: {canary_string[:16]}"
    out = model_fn(probe)
    return canary_string[16:32] in out          # True -> likely contaminated

def generate_dynamic_task(template, rng):
    """Instantiate a fresh task from a template so it cannot be memorised."""
    a, b = rng.randint(100, 999), rng.randint(100, 999)
    question = template.format(a=a, b=b)
    answer = str(a * b)                          # verifiable, never in training data
    task_id = hashlib.md5(question.encode()).hexdigest()[:8]
    return {"id": task_id, "question": question, "answer": answer}

def build_fresh_suite(template, n=200, seed=0):
    rng = random.Random(seed)
    return [generate_dynamic_task(template, rng) for _ in range(n)]
Tasks with a single correct answer
Efficiency and safety of the path
Rigorous evaluation is the foundation of trustworthy progress, but rigour is expensive and can itself distort behavior if a metric becomes a target.
— many runs with intervals let you tell real improvement from sampling noise.
— trajectory attribution turns an opaque score into an actionable diagnosis.
— sound evaluation is the gate that keeps unreliable agents out of production.
— statistical power demands many full trajectories, which is expensive.
— LLM judges introduce position, verbosity, and self-preference biases.
— optimising against any fixed benchmark eventually stops measuring generalisation.
— a bare success rate on a small suite hides intervals that likely overlap a rival's.
— contamination inflates scores; a high number may reflect memorisation.
— outcome-only misses unsafe method; judge-only inherits model bias; combine them.
— without a rubric, judges reward confident, verbose prose over correctness.
— non-determinism means one run cannot rank two agents.
Run each task many times and report success rates with confidence intervals.
Combine outcome checks, trajectory analysis, and rubric-guided judging.
Keep test sets private and rotate or dynamically generate tasks to fight contamination.
Attribute failures to trajectory stages so fixes target the real weak link.
Calibrate automated metrics against human judgement periodically.
a high benchmark score means a capable agent.
Contamination and metric-gaming inflate scores; a high number on a public, possibly leaked benchmark can reflect memorisation, not capability.
an LLM judge is an objective grader.
Judges have position, verbosity, and self-preference biases; they need rubrics, bias mitigation, and calibration against humans.
one run is enough to compare two agents.
Non-determinism makes single runs noisy; sound comparison needs many runs and overlapping-interval checks.
Agent evaluation must judge trajectories and outcomes, not just final strings, because tasks are open-ended and multi-path.
No single method suffices: combine outcome checks, trajectory analysis, and rubric-guided LLM judging.
Non-determinism demands many runs and confidence intervals; point estimates on small suites are unsound.
Contamination silently inflates public benchmarks; fresh, held-out, or dynamic tasks are needed.
A flawed metric misdirects development and can certify an unreliable agent as ready — guard the evaluation itself.
